# Transcrição: 04-Charlie s Second Circumscion

- **Fonte:** 04-Charlie s Second Circumscion
- **Duração:** 05:55
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

I'm standing next to my wife, Alicia. We're looking down, we're staring really. And then finally she says, I don't like the looks of that penis. And I'm just so happy that we're not looking at my penis at the moment. The problem is we're looking at my son's penis, Charlie. He's nine months old and she is right. There's something wrong with this penis. I've known it for a long time. I've seen this problem and I've just been waiting for it to sort of resolve itself. It's the way I like to solve problems. I pretend they're not there and maybe eventually they will go away. Not his penis, just the problem with his penis. But it hasn't gone away. Charlie is circumcised, which means when he's 26 years old, he's going to come home fully woke and tell us that we have generally mutilated him. And he won't be wrong. That is what we did. But I will blame his mother because she is Jewish. And somewhere along the way, her people decided that foreskin and religion get all intertwined and it is necessary for her people to do this thing. But we've also circumcised him because most American men are circumcised. And we wanted Charlie to sort of look like everybody else. His last name is already Dix. So he's in for a lifetime of dick jokes. We didn't want to put a hat on a hat and create even more of a problem. So I say, what are we going to do when we go to the doctor? So Charlie's on the examining table and the doctor is looking at Charlie's penis. And he turns and he says, well, you could circumcise again or not, whichever you want. This is not what I want to hear. I don't bring my child to medical experts so they can throw the problem back on my lap. So as we're driving home, I ask Alicia, what are we going to do? And she says the two most beautiful words I've ever heard. Second opinion. So a month later, we're at New York Presbyterian and we are with the king of all penis doctors. And he is looking at Charlie's penis. He sees it and he says, if this was my son, I would re-circumcise immediately. And I am thrilled that this is the solution. Like, I love the fact that an expert is going to take responsibility for this decision. Because I really don't want responsibility for this decision. I know it's just like a penis and they're funny in very many ways. But it's also surgery. And surgery means anesthesia. And anesthesia means you go to sleep. And we almost always wake up from anesthesia. But sometimes we don't. And I am not brave enough to make a decision like this for my son. And so I am happy to slough it off on some guy who knows more than me. So a month later, we're at New York Presbyterian and the nurse is explaining how the situation is going to work. It's very complicated. We're going to go up to the surgical floor. There's going to be two nurses, my wife, myself, and my son. And when we get up to the floor, one parent and the nurse will wait by the elevator. And the other parent will take Charlie into the OR and they will stay with Charlie until he's asleep.

## [03:03 - 05:55]

And then that parent will come out too. And they say, which parent's going to go into the operating room? I do not want to go in the operating room. I am not courageous enough to handle that situation. But I also know my wife wants to go. Which means if I say I want to go, she will think I'm brave. And then when I relent, I will get marriage points for allowing her to do the thing I don't want to do to begin with. So I say, I want to go in the operating room. And she says, I really want to go. And I try to calculate that time that a decent human being would take to sort of weigh the options before finally relenting. And after those seconds tick by, I say, all right, you can be the one. So we go up the elevator and I stand by the elevator with my nurse. And I watch Alicia walk across this lobby to the operating room with her nurse. She's already crying before she even gets into the operating room. And I'm just so happy it's her and it's not me. Because I'm fine. Everything is good on my end. And then she comes out of the operating room a little while later and she's still crying. She is weeping. And she's on the other side of the lobby and the nurse is sort of filling out some form. And Alicia is standing next to her and I'm watching. And then my nurse leans in and says, this is the moment that husbands usually comfort their wives. And now I know why she's standing with me. The whole system makes sense now. And so I go across the lobby and I take my wife and my arms and the instant I'm holding her, I understand everything that has happened. A big decision need to be made about our son. And I passed off the responsibility to another person, to an expert who I trusted. Because I wasn't brave enough to make that decision for my son. But my wife didn't do that. She listened to the experts and she made a decision for herself and for Charlie. She held on to the responsibility that I could not. And I knew it because the moment I'm holding her, she is a ball of stress and knots. And I am the most relaxed person in the room. And I suddenly understand what I have done to my wife. I have not done my job as a parent. I have sloughed it off to another person because I am not good enough to handle this at this time. And so I'm holding my wife there in that little lobby. And I'm thinking about how it's going to be a long time before I am a better parent. A long time before I'm the father I've always wanted to be. But it occurs to me holding her that it is time for me to start being a much better husband. Thank you.
